howItWorks.intro: This wind turbine calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Power = ½ × Air Density × Swept Area × Wind Speed³ × Efficiency
Annual Output = Rated Power × 8,760 hours × Capacity Factor
howItWorks.outro: Energy costs and incentives change by region and season. Check with your local utility for current rates.

faq.question: How much power does a small wind turbine produce?
faq.answer: A small residential turbine (10-15 ft rotor) in 12 mph average winds produces 2,000-5,000 kWh/year — about 20-40% of average US home usage. Output scales with the cube of wind speed.

faq.question: What wind speed do I need for a wind turbine?
faq.answer: Minimum average of 9-10 mph for viability. 12+ mph average is ideal. Wind resources vary greatly even within small areas — get a proper wind assessment before investing.

faq.question: Is a home wind turbine worth it?
faq.answer: Depends on wind resource, electricity costs, and incentives. In good wind areas (12+ mph avg) with high electricity rates ($0.15+/kWh), payback can be 10-15 years. Tax credits can cut this significantly.

faq.question: Is the Wind Turbine Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Wind Turbine Calculator?
sections.body: The Wind Turbine Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your average wind speed, rotor diameter, system cost, you get instant results including rated power, annual energy output, monthly savings. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting rated power right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your average wind speed and need to find the right rated power. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Average Wind Speed (mph)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your average wind speed — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: rotor diameter, system cost, electricity rate. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your rated power, annual energy output, monthly savings. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your average wind speed before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both rated power and annual energy output — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your electricity rate, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
